coredata.dc:title: Microwave bottom-up route for size-tunable and switchable photoluminescent graphene quantum dots using acetylacetone: New platform for enzyme-free detection of hydrogen peroxide
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Umrao, Sima
Jang, Min-Ho
Oh, Jung-Hwan
Kim, Guntae
Sahoo, Sumanta
Cho, Yong-Hoon
Srivastva, Anchal
Oh, Il-Kwon
coredata.dc:description: We report a microwave sequential bottom-up route to produce green and blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (g-GQDs and b-GQDs) with size-tunable and switchable functionalities by tailoring the diameter size and functional groups via microwave carbonization and aromatization processes from acetylacetone as a starting organic solvent. The b-GQDs as the final product show only one emission peak at 433nm and pH-independent blue luminescence, because two-step microwave irradiation could reduce the size and the oxygen-functional groups of the g-GQDs as an intermediate product. Also, the b-GQDs provide an exemplar enzyme-free platform for hydrogen peroxide detection through the electrochemical sensing due to much higher electron density and electron donating properties. In contrast, the g-GQDs show two different switchable photoluminescent emissions at ∼460nm (P1) and ∼500nm (P2): the P1 emission with sky-blue fluorescence originates from randomly conjugated oxygen-functional groups on the basal plane and/or edge of the g-GQDs and the P2 emission with green fluorescence results from quasi-molecular fluorophores formed by the electronic coupling of carboxylic acid groups.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and spectroscopic characterization of as synthesised GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: International Cooperation of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korean Government’s Ministry of Knowledge Economy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the International Cooperation of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) , in a grant funded by the Korean Government’s Ministry of Knowledge Economy (No. 20128510010050 ) and a National Research Foundation of Korea grant funded by the Korean Government ( 2012R1A2A2A01047543 ). I.O. and Y.C. were supported by GRC project of KI for the NanoCentury. S.U. acknowledges KAIST and Banaras Hindu University (BHU) for giving permission to work at KAIST as a visiting research fellow. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of microwave bottom-up route for g-GQDs and b-GQDs: green circles mean carboxyl and carbonyl groups and blue circles indicate hydroxyl groups. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of (a) g-GQDs and (b) b-GQDs and corresponding HRTEM image in right side. AFM images of (c) g-GQDs and (d) b-GQDs (The scale bar inside AFM images is 1μm). Height distributions of (e) g-GQDs and (f) b-GQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Raman spectra of g-GQDs and b-GQDs, (b) FTIR spectra of acetylacetone and as-synthesized GQDs, (c) XPS C1s spectra of g-GQDs, and (d) XPS C1s spectra of b-GQDs; CC (dark cyan), CC (magenta), CO (orange), COOH (dark yellow), and COH (cyan). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photoluminescence characterizations of g-GQDs: (a) 2D matrices from the PL spectra of g-GQDs with pH 7 under various excitation wavelengths. P1 and P2 represent the emission peak positions in g-GQDs. (b) Normalized PL and PL excitation spectra of P1 (upper) and P2 (below) in inset optical image of g-GQDs under corresponding excitation light. (c) Time evolution of PL intensity of P1 (upper) and P2 (below) in g-GQDs with λ ex =370 and 470nm, and PL decay curves were measured at an emission wavelength range of 46,010nm and 50,010nm for P1 and P2, respectively. Emission wavelength matrices from the PL spectra of g-GQDs with (d) pH 1 and (e) pH 12 under various excitation wavelengths. (f) Normalized PL and PL excitation spectra of P1 in pH 1 (upper) and pH 12 (below). PL and PLE of pH 1 (pH 12) represent λ ex at 350nm (370nm) and λ de at 440nm (460nm), respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photoluminescence characterizations of b-GQDs: (a) 2D matrices from the PL spectra of b-GQDs in pH 7 under various excitation wavelengths. The P3 represents the emission peak position in b-GQDs. (b) Normalized PL and PL excitation spectra at P3. PL and PLE of P3 represent an excitation wavelength (λ ex) at 325nm and a detection wavelength (λ de) at 440nm, respectively, in the inset optical image of b-GQDs under UV light (325nm). (c) Time evolution of PL intensity from b-GQDs with λ ex =300nm and PL decay curves detected at an emission wavelength range of 43,010nm. (d) Normalized PL spectra excited by 325nm under various pH solvents; pH 7 (black-line), pH 1 (red-line), and pH 12 (blue-line). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Enzyme-free electrochemical sensing of H2O2: (a) Cyclic voltammograms of the bare GCE and GQDs-embedded GCE electrodes in oxygen-free PBS (0.1M, pH 7) at the scan rate of 0.2mV/s with 1mM H2O2. (b) Amperometric responses of the b-GQDs-embedded GCE to the successive addition of H2O2 1mM to 10mM. Inset shows amperometric response for lower concentration range from 0.02mM to 1mM of H2O2. (c) Cyclic voltammograms for b-GQDs-embedded GCE with different scan rates from 30mV/s to 200mV/s with 1mM H2O2 in 0.1M PBS at pH 7. (d) The corresponding plot of reduction current peak versus the square root of scan rate for b-GQDs-embedded GCE. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Microwave bottom-up route for size-tunable and switchable photoluminescent graphene quantum dots using acetylacetone: New platform for enzyme-free detection of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graphene Research Center, KAIST Institute for the NanoCentury, School of Mechanical, Aerospace and Systems Engineering, Division of Ocean Systems Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-701, Republic of Korea

Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Graphene Research Center, KAIST Institute for the NanoCentury, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-701, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Graphene Research Center
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a microwave sequential bottom-up route to produce green and blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (g-GQDs and b-GQDs) with size-tunable and switchable functionalities by tailoring the diameter size and functional groups via microwave carbonization and aromatization processes from acetylacetone as a starting organic solvent. The b-GQDs as the final product show only one emission peak at 433nm and pH-independent blue luminescence, because two-step microwave irradiation could reduce the size and the oxygen-functional groups of the g-GQDs as an intermediate product. Also, the b-GQDs provide an exemplar enzyme-free platform for hydrogen peroxide detection through the electrochemical sensing due to much higher electron density and electron donating properties. In contrast, the g-GQDs show two different switchable photoluminescent emissions at ∼460nm (P1) and ∼500nm (P2): the P1 emission with sky-blue fluorescence originates from randomly conjugated oxygen-functional groups on the basal plane and/or edge of the g-GQDs and the P2 emission with green fluorescence results from quasi-molecular fluorophores formed by the electronic coupling of carboxylic acid groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been newly recognized as one of the essential nano-materials due to their unique and startling properties such as quantum confinement, tunable band gap, stable fluorescence, high surface area, and high electrical conductivity [1–10]. By modulating their size, surfaces and edges, the band gap and photoluminescences of GQDs can be also tailored for a specified target application. Additionally, GQDs are empowered with low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility, chemical inertia, and good surface grafting, thus making them exemplar in the fields of optoelectronics, sensors and bio-imaging [2,11–14].

The most fascinating feature of GQDs is their photoluminescence (PL). The PL phenomena of GQDs have been qualitatively explained with exciton of carbon, emissive traps, quantum confinement, aromatic structures, oxygen-containing functional groups, free zigzag sites and edge defects [1,15]. However, a meticulous quantitative explanation is beyond the ken of present knowledge and the exact mechanism of GQDs remains unsettled. Until now, various synthetic routes of fabricating GQDs have been intensively investigated including top-down [11–21] and bottom-up [22–26] approaches. Methods of cutting carbon fibers [17], graphene sheet and graphene oxide [4,16,20] are classified into the top-down method. The bottom-up strategy involves the formation of GQDs via stepwise solution chemistry of oxidative condensation reactions, via molecular precursor with cage-opening of fullerene [25], via carbonizing of some special organic precursor like glucose or citric acid using hydrothermal treatment [22,24], and via reduction of a pre-synthesized polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon hexa-peri-hexabenzo-coronene precursor [23]. However, some bottom-up routes reported before release toxic and harmful gases and waste, requiring tedious and additional process steps to resolve much concerned negative ecological aspects [23–25,27].

For the practical engineering applications, the PL of GQDs should be controlled by tailoring the size [16,17], zigzag and/or armchair distribution [12], and residual defects of GQDs [3,18,19]. However, some previous methods are not suitable for the precise control of these parameters because of a nonselective chemical cutting, a random reduction process, and complicated multistep processes. Therefore, there is a need for a better prototype of the synthetic route, which not only aids easy, economical, ecological and reproducible production of the GQDs, but also allows for a precise control over the size and emission-color of the GQDs. Until now, fluorescence has been extensively applied to various sensors including biosensors. Recent reports manifest GQDs as a potential material for efficient and selective electrochemical sensing, by utilizing better electron accepter and transporter of GQDs [29]. Most GQD biosensors require enzymes or cross-linkers to detect specified chemicals and biomolecules. But, the cross-link binders can critically degrade the durability and life of biosensors by the contamination of the GQD sensor and uncontrolled desorption mechanism [5,29]. Carbon quantum dots also was used to develop a sensitive fluorescent sensor for H2O2 detection in aqueous media by quenching in fluorescent intensity [30]. The graphene and glassy carbon electrode can exhibit fast electron transfer for Fe(CN)6 3−/4− redox couple and can be used for a sensitive detection of the enzyme-free detection of H2O2. So, the enzyme-free detection of some chemicals and biomolecules can be a challenging topic for reliable and repeatable GQD biosensors.

Herein, we report a novel microwave assisted bottom-up method to produce functionally switchable and size-tunable GQDs by microwave carbonization and aromatization processes of a simple and economical organic solvent, acetylacetone (AcAc). Due to the weak acidic nature of AcAc, the dehydration and decomposition reactions proceed in a controlled manner under microwave irradiation and subsequently the formation of aromatic clusters take place via aldol condensation in the presence of H+ as a catalyst and cycloaddition as described in Supplementary information. Our two-step sequential route to produce a green PL emitting GQD (g-GQD) and a blue PL emitting GQD (b-GQD) provides a succinct control over the size and functional groups by modulating microwave power and irradiation time as shown in Fig. 1 . However, the AcAc starting material does not have a distinct PL emission from 350nm to 500nm, as shown in Fig. S1. The g-GQDs having carbonyl and carboxyl groups exhibit quasi-molecular fluorophores as an intermediate product with narrow green emission band width, while b-GQDs having specific hydroxyl functional groups exalt the blue luminescence with splendid functionalities of pH-independence and enzyme-free detection of H2O2.

Acetylacetone (99% purity), Sodium phosphate monobasic (NaH2PO4, 98%), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%), and Phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 99.99%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All of the chemical were used as received.

GQDs were prepared by microwave irradiation of organic solvent AcAc, which has a weak acidic nature. 50ml of acetylacetone was placed in a quartz bowl with 2 drops of added DI water, and after the cover was tightened it was put into a microwave oven. Microwave irradiation with temperature limit of 200°C and power of 800W was performed for 5min until the colorless solution of acetylacetone was changed into a brownish-yellow sticky material on the surface of bowl. Then, the brownish-yellow sticky material was cooled down to room temperature, and the resulting product was dissolved in water. Finally, an aqueous suspension of g-GQDs was obtained after centrifugation at 8000rpm for 5min to remove the unreacted organic moieties. Subsequently, we used a 2nd step of microwave radiation with the power of 900W to get b-GQDs.

To fabricate a GQDs-embedded glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for the electrochemical detection of H2O2, 10μL of GQDs solution (0.5mg/mL) was dropped onto a pre-polished mirror-like GCE surface and dried thoroughly in air. Then, 2μL of Nafion solution (20wt%) was used as a fixative to form a strong film to modify the electrode.

All detection measurements were performed under similar condition. Briefly, 0.2mg/mL solution of each type of GQDs was used for electrocatalytic tests. All electrochemical experiments were carried out using the three-electrode system, the GQDs-embedded GCE was used as the working electrode. And a platinum electrode and a standard calomel electrode were used as the counter electrode and a reference electrode, respectively. Moreover, the solutions of H2O2 with varying concentrations were added into the PBS solution under stirring condition in each cyclic-voltammetry run, respectively.

The morphology of the GQDs was examined using transmission electron microscopes (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin). The height of the GQDs samples was characterized by using atomic force microscope (AFM) (N8 NEOS Senterra) in non-contact mode. High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were determined using an ESCA Multilab 200 system. Raman spectra were obtained with an ARAMIS. High Power Normal & Micro X-ray Diffractometer (D/MAX-2500(18kW), RIGAKU), Multi-Purpose X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) (Sigma Probe, Thermo VG Scientific), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) Spectrometer (FT-IR 4100, Jasco) and UV–vis-NIR Spectrometer (V-570, Jasco) were used for characterization of the as-synthesized GQDs. Monochromatic light from a 300W Xenon lamp, dispersed by a monochromator, was focused on the samples for PL and PLE experiments with low-excitation power (<0.1mW) at room temperature. The photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were detected by UV spectrometers (Maya2000, Ocean Optics, USA) and a high-sensitive photomultiplier tube detector at room temperature. The quantum yields (QYs) were measured by an absolute PL QYs measurement system (C9920-02G, HAMAMATSU), which estimates total photon flux through an integration sphere. In order to elucidate the recombination dynamics, we carried out time-resolved PL experiments. A mode-locked femto-second pulsed Ti:sapphire laser (Coherent, Chameleon Ultra II) system was used as an excitation source, and the three wavelengths of the pulsed Ti:sapphire laser (300nm, 370nm, and 470nm) were employed. A streak camera (Hamamatsu, C7700-01) was utilized to measure the decay profile of the PL spectra at room temperature. All electrochemical experiments were carried out on a multichannel potentiostat/galvanostat (VersaStat, Princeton Applied Research) at room temperature. In the three electrode system, the GQDs-embedded GCE was used as the working electrode, platinum as the counter electrode, and a standard calomel electrode as a reference electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and spectroscopic characterization of as synthesised GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a major ingredient, blue and green luminescent GQDs were synthesized by one step microwave assisted method using organic solvent acetylacetone (AcAc) as a precursor as shown in Fig. 1. See supplementary information for the detailed explanation. The morphological shape and size of the as-synthesized g-GQDs and b-GQDs were characterized by TEM, HRTEM and AFM as shown in Fig. 2 .

The g-GQDs have a diameter of 5.0±0.5nm, while the b-GQDs have a diameter of 2.3±0.5nm as shown in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. The magnified Fig. 1a and b are the HRTEM images, which indicate high crystallinity for both GQDs with interlayer spacing of 0.39nm for g-GQDs and 0.35nm for b-GQDs. The AFM images (Fig. 1c and d) and the height profiles reveal that the heights of the g-GQDs and b-GQDs are 2–4nm and 0.9–2.0nm, respectively, as shown in Figs. 2e and 1f. The height distribution of all samples is greater than 1nm; that is, the species are much higher than normal polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons like acetylacetone [31]. This confirms that the hydrocarbons change into quantum dots.

The Raman spectrum of the as-synthesized g-GQDs shows two typical peaks at 1358 and 1595cm−1 in Fig. 3 a, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of graphene, respectively. The G-band is due to E2g mode at the Γ-point, arising from the stretching in sp2 hybridized carbon, bonded either with neighboring carbon atoms or with oxygen in the form of carboxyl and carbonyl groups.

The D band at 1358cm−1, which is a prominent feature in the spectrum, indicates the creation of sp3 domains due to the extensive oxidation [32]. On the other hand, the D and G bands of the b-GQDs were slightly shifted to 1371 and 1631cm−1. In case of b-GQDs, the G-band broadens and shifts towards higher wavenumber due to the increase in the amount of carbon, bonded to either neighboring carbon or with oxygen in the form of epoxy and hydroxyl group via sp3 hybridisation [33]. The I D/I G ratios which is a measure of crystal size are ∼1.08 (for g-GQDs) and ∼0.41 (for b-GQDs), revealing that b-GQDs have smaller crystal size compared with g-GQDs. The decrease in crystal size indicates the restoration of the aromatic carbon structure and the removal of carbonyl and carboxyl functional groups in the g-GQDs after the two-step microwave irradiation.

In the XRD pattern, as shown in Fig. S3, the diffraction peak is shifted from 22.9° (g-GQDs) to 25.2° (b-GQDs), corresponding to (002) inter planar spacing of 0.39nm and 0.35nm for the g-GQDs and b-GQDs, respectively. The peak of the b-GQDs is closer to the (002) graphite peak of 0.342nm, suggesting that more carbonization of AcAc can produce graphitic structures. The broad band (002) peak show crystallization, with crystals that have dimension of 1.47nm and 1.20nm, which are consistent with the mean distance between two defects of the GQDs in the Raman spectroscopy results as shown in Fig. 2e. The change of aliphatic carbons into the aromatic structure of GQDs was confirmed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 3b; we can easily recognize that the various functional groups are present in the GQDs. The aliphatic CC bond, blue-shifted by 12cm−1, and the CO bond, red-shifted by ∼23cm−1, make aromatic CC and CO bonds in the g-GQDs. The FTIR spectrum of the g-GQDs shows the presence of CC stretching (1625cm−1), CO (1731cm−1), CO (1112cm−1) and a broad peak at 3464cm−1 due to the adsorbed water content [34]. Most importantly, the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1731cm−1 only appear in the g-GQDs, but it disappears in the b-GQDs, indicating that the b-GQDs may not have carbonyl (CO) and carboxyl (COOH) groups after the 2nd microwave irradiation and have a new OH bending band at 1380cm−1. We employed XPS for further confirmation of reduction of functional group in case of b-GQDs. Fig. S2 shows that the oxygen content in the g-GQDs is 35.7%, which is reduced significantly in case of b-GQDs ca. 10.83%. As shown in Fig. 3c, the deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of g-GQDs have four components at 284.6eV, 285.2eV, 286.9eV, and 289.4eV, corresponding to sp2 aromatic carbon (CC), sp3 aromatic carbon (CC/CH), carbonyl group and carboxyl group, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks assigned to the carbonyl group and carboxyl group were clearly absent in the case of the b-GQDs as shown in Fig. 3d. But, hydroxyl (COH) groups appear in the XPS data of b-GQDs. Therefore, both g-GQDs and b-GQDs show good water-solubility which can be attributed to the introduction of hydrophilic functionalities.

The photophysical properties of the GQDs are the key to their future applications in the fields of optoelectronic devices and biological sensors [6,7,35,36]. To further explore the photophysical properties of the as-synthesized GQDs, the excitation wavelength dependent PL, PL excitation (PLE), and time-resolved PL (TRPL) experiments were carried out for various conditions. We tried to reveal the fluorescence mechanism in order to analyze the relationship between the surface chemical structures and the corresponding PL behaviors. Fig. 3a, d, and e under three different pH conditions show two-dimensional (2D) PL intensity map of the g-GQDs as a function of PL emission wavelength (λ em) under various excitation wavelengths (λ ex). The interesting finding is that the 2D matrices of the g-GQDs in the pH 7 solvent exhibit two distinct PL peaks, labeled P1 and P2, under different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 4 a. Fig. 4b presents the normalized PL and PLE spectra of the P1 and P2 positions in 2D matrices of the g-GQDs in pH 7 solvent. The emission maxima of P1 and P2 are at ∼460nm and ∼500nm, respectively, and the emission-colors of the PL spectra were sky-blue (0.23, 0.29) and green (0.27, 0.60) in the international commission on illumination (CIE) map shown in Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. 3b, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of P1 (∼0.85eV) is significantly wider than that of P2 (∼0.31eV).

The peaks of the PLE spectra of P1 and P2 are located at ∼370nm and ∼ 485nm, respectively. To obtain the absolute quantum yield (QY) accurately, we used an absolute PL quantum yield measurement system [37], and measured the QYs of P1 and P2 excited by 370nm and 480nm, respectively, corresponding to the peak of the PLE spectra. The QYs of P1 and P2 were measured as 1.0% and 3.4%, respectively.

In further study of optical properties of P1 and P2, we found different PL decay profiles from both of them, as shown in the Fig. 4c. The P1 shows the non-exponential PL decay, indicating that the emission is associated with several types of excited energy levels, whereas the P2 emission is nearly a single exponential. The very different PL, PLE, and TRPL results suggest that the luminescence of P1 and P2 emissions arises from two different types of electronically excited states in the g-GQDs. Typically, the PLE spectrum of GQDs with pure sp2 carbon bonding (i.e. pristine-GQDs [38] and reduced-GQDs [4,19]) shows a sharp peak of π–π∗ transition in a narrow range from 250nm to 300nm. Although the UV–vis absorbance of the g-GQDs shows a main ∗peak around 216nm comes from the π–π∗ transition of CC and CC bonds as shown in Fig. S5a, a sharp peak of π–π∗ transition was not found in the PLE spectra in Fig. 3b. On the other hand, the PLE spectrum of the g-GQDs shows a broad peak at ∼370nm, which corresponds to a shoulder peak related to oxygen-functional groups in UV–vis absorbance (Fig. S5a). Hence, we propose that both the P1 and P2 luminescences of g-GQDs are mainly associated with various oxygen-functional groups.

To understand the origins of luminescence in g-GQDs, we prepared the g-GQDs in different pH solvents, and obtained the 2D PL matrices of g-GQDs under pH 1 and 12 as shown in Fig. 3d and e. Under the acid condition (pH∼1) shown in Fig. 4d, two distinct PL peaks were observed in the 2D PL matrices, like that of pH 7. The peak position of Pl had blue-shifted from 460nm to 425nm, while the P2 at 500nm did not change significantly compared to that of pH 7. On the other hand, in the 2D PL matrices for pH 12 condition as shown in Fig. 4e, we only observed one peak, corresponding to the P1 under the pH 7 condition. In other words, the P2 peak observed at 500nm under the neutral condition of pH 7 clearly disappears in the 2D PL matrices under pH 12 as shown in Fig. 4e. The unpaired electrons can contribute to electron donations from functional groups to the GQDs in a manner like the electron-withdrawing and electron-accepting behaviors of functional groups to benzene, which are well-known behaviors in organic chemistry [18]. For this reason, Jin et al. [18], reported that the PL peak shift of GQDs under different pH condition could be attributed to charge transfer by the protonation and/or deprotonation of functional groups. Based on the results of PLE, TRPL, and the variation of PL with varying pH solvent, the P1 emission in g-GQDs originates from randomly conjugated oxygen-functional groups on the basal plane and/or edge of the g-GQDs, like previously reported GQDs [3,18]. In addition, the P1 emission agrees well with the shift of the PL peak position increased with increasing excitation wavelength, shown in Fig. S6a, and this is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based QDs [3,4,7,13,19,22–24,36]. On the other hand, compared with previously reported GQDs, the spectra of P2 in Fig. 4b clearly show sharp emission peaks (FWHM=∼0.31eV), narrow fluorescence Stokes shifts, and sensitive pH-dependence under pH 12 as well as a constant PL peak position changed by excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. S6b. These P2 properties resemble data from a molecular fluorophore [39]. Therefore, the P2 emission in g-GQDs can be attributed to quasi-molecular fluorophores, which are formed by the electronic coupling of carboxylic and carbonyl groups for an appropriate chemical reaction.

To further clarify the feature by the surface chemistry of the GQDs, we confirmed the optical properties of b-GQDs, which had been produced by the 2nd microwave irradiation on g-GQDs with much higher microwave power. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the b-GQDs show only one blue emission peak, labeled P3, at 433nm under an excitation wavelength of 325nm, because the additional microwave irradiation reduces the carbonyl and carboxyl oxygen-functional groups of the g-GQDs, as confirmed by FTIR and XPS analysis. Therefore, a quasi-molecular emission could not be observed in b-GQDs, compared with that of g-GQDs [3,40,41]. In the case of the P3 in b-GQDs, the PL decay curve (Fig. 5c) is non-exponential similar to the P1 emission of g-GQDs. In addition, the optical properties of b-GQDs based on pH-dependence resemble the results of P1 in g-GQDs, exhibiting a blue-shift and a non-shift of the PL peak position under pH conditions of 1 (red-line) and 12 (green-line), respectively, as shown in Fig. 5d. Therefore, the origins of the emission in b-GQDs are very similar to that of P1 in g-GQDs, and are attributed to randomly remaining oxygen-functional groups on the basal plane and/or edge of g-GQDs. Further, both the PL and PLE spectra in Fig. 5b were blue-shifted about 30nm and 50nm, respectively, compared to that of P1 in g-GQDs (Fig. 4b), respectively. Based on the TEM, AFM, XPS, and FTIR results, we already know that the b-GQDs have a smaller size and lower number of oxygen-functional groups than the g-GQDs. It has been reported that both features commonly result in increasing the band gap in GQDs [3,17,19,38]. This suggests that the blue emission of b-GQDs relative to that of P1 in g-GQDs results from the smaller size and fewer oxygen-functional groups.
In this work, we modulated the emission-color by changing the size and number of oxygen-functional groups in the GQDs, by controlling the power and time of microwave irradiation.

The strong green emission has been reported in graphene oxide and GQDs, but it has had abroad emission bandwidth, larger than ∼100nm. Until now, however, it has been difficult to obtain a narrow green emission due to the very complex and/or randomly conjugated oxygen-functional groups. We were able to obtain quasi-molecular fluorophores in our GQDs which resulted in a narrow green emission (FWHM=∼0.31eV, ∼65nm) and higher QYs than the emission originating from randomly conjugated oxygen-functional groups, as well as a very sensitive pH-solvent condition.

GQDs are recognized as better electron transporters and accepters, and these features make GQDs a better platform for electrochemical sensing and cellular imaging [4,28,42,46]. The sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is important because it is widely used in many fields such as food, environment and biological analysis. The accurate detection of H2O2 is of interest in both biomedical and environmental particularly in biosensing field [43]. H2O2 is generated as byproduct of a biological process, as a product of incomplete reduction of O2 [44]. It is also known to be involved in the function and signal transduction of the cells [45–47]. A non-enzymatic H2O2 sensor was prepared by the direct deposition of GQDs on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). However, most GQD biosensors require enzymes or cross-linkers to detect specified chemicals and biomolecules [43–47]. Therefore, we explored the potential application of g-GQDs and b-GQDs in enzyme-free H2O2 sensing, by utilizing electrochemical sensing without any other cross-linkers. A non-enzymatic H2O2 sensor was prepared by the direct deposition of GQDs on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Fig. 6 a shows the electrocatalytic responses of the GQD-embedded GCE electrode and the bare GCE electrode toward the reduction of H2O2 in oxygen free 0.1M PBS solution at pH 7.4. In the presence of H2O2, the base GCE does not show electrochemical responses towards the reduction of H2O2. In contrast, g-GQDs and b-GQDs exhibit a remarkable response and a reductive peak appears at around −0.35V. The present results show that b-GQDs are better than g-GQDs from the viewpoint of their electrochemical responses, due to fewer oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces, resulting in an increase of electron transfer in between GCE and b-GQDs.

The reduction peak current is exceptionally high in comparison with reported GQDs-embedded Au electrodes using cystamine as cross-linker [5], indicating that the reduction of H2O2 increases in case of b-GQDs. In the electrochemical measurement, the chemical reaction for sensing H2O2 is given as:
(1)
GQDs →
GQDs
+
+
e
-
(2)
H
2
O
2
+ 2
e
-
→ 2
OH
-

Fig. 6b shows the corresponding plot of reduction peak current versus H2O2 concentration for the b-GQDs-embedded GCE. It shows a linear increase in current with increasing concentration from 1mM to 10mM with a lower detection limit of 0.02mM. The inset graph illustrates the lower concentration range of 0.02mM to 1mM. These results indicate that the b-GQDs-embedded GCE exhibits a linear range and a low detection limit of H2O2. In addition, the effects of potential and scan rate on H2O2 sensing were also investigated as shown in Fig. 6c. The reduction current of the b-GQDs increased linearly with the increase in the scan rate from 30mV/s to 200mV/s. The reduction current increases linearly with the square root of the scan rate as shown in Fig. 6d, indicating a diffusion-controlled process [43]. The sensitive detection of H2O2 based on strong reduction at a negative applied potential has the potential to eliminate interferences from other electroactive compounds. The b-GQDs-embedded GCE gives a fast and high reduction current even in very low applied potential, i.e. ∼0.03V, with different concentrations of H2O2 see Fig. S7 for data on the g-GQDs-embedded GCE. The good electrochemical properties of the non-enzymatic electrode indicate that the electron density in b-GQDs is higher and can enhance electron donating properties even at a very low scan rate. Therefore, the b-GQDs-embedded GCE shows a very high reduction current in comparison with the g-GQDs-embedded GCE, because a significant number of electrons in g-GQDs are trapped due to the higher number of oxygen groups on their surfaces. These preliminary results open further potential applications of GQDs in medical and biological research, such as the detection of human breast cancer causing adenocarcinoma cell line MCF-7 to release H2O2.

Distilling the above work, we developed a novel ultrafast, economic, ecological and reproducible microwave-assisted strategy to produce blue and green luminescent graphene quantum dots (b-GQDs and g-GQDs) from an organic solvent, acetylacetone, as the starting material, evading the drawback of most bottom-up approaches reported till date. Alternative two-step microwave process is suitable for the precise control over the size of GQDs and switchable photoluminescence functionality of GQDs. As the final product, the b-GQDs with diameter of 2.3nm only have hydroxyl (COH) functional groups without carbonyl and carboxyl groups, resulting in one emission peak at 433nm and pH-insensitive blue photoluminescence. Most interestingly, b-GQDs can be an exemplar platform for enzyme-free detection of H2O2 due to much higher electron density and electron donating properties. In contrast, the g-GQDs show two different switchable photoluminescent (PL) emissions at ∼460nm (P1) and ∼500nm (P2): the P1 emission with sky-blue fluorescence originates from randomly conjugated oxygen-functional groups on the basal plane and/or edge of the g-GQDs and the P2 emission with green fluorescence results from quasi-molecular fluorophores formed by the electronic coupling of carboxylic acid groups. Furthermore, g-GQDs clearly show pH dependent PL behavior; the P1 emission under acidic solvent conditions is blue shifted due to the charge transfer and the P2 emission disappears under basic solvent condition. In a nutshell, we have successfully tailored the size and functional groups to synthesize switchable and functionally tunable GQDs with unique novel properties using a relatively simple and fast bottom-up route which has a potential to act as an archetype for future applications in enzyme-free biosensors, bio-imaging, and optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the International Cooperation of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), in a grant funded by the Korean Government’s Ministry of Knowledge Economy (No. 20128510010050) and a National Research Foundation of Korea grant funded by the Korean Government (2012R1A2A2A01047543). I.O. and Y.C. were supported by GRC project of KI for the NanoCentury. S.U. acknowledges KAIST and Banaras Hindu University (BHU) for giving permission to work at KAIST as a visiting research fellow.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.09.084.
